channel.title: NYT > Travel
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: 5 New Stockholm Bakeries
item.description: The Swedish appetite for sweets, caffeine and hanging out has led to a profusion of new convivial bakeries.
item.dc:creator: Ingrid K. Williams and Sofia Runarsdotter
item.media:credit: Sofia Runarsdotter for The New York Times

item.title: Spirit Airlines Is Shutting Down. Here’s What to Do if You’re Scheduled to Fly.
item.description: After two stints in bankruptcy, the low-cost airline shut down early Saturday. Here’s what travelers should know about rebooking and refunds.
item.dc:creator: Seth Kugel
item.media:description: Spirit Airlines, squeezed by spiking jet fuel prices, shut down on Saturday after a proposed government bailout fell apart.

item.title: ‘Mr. Nobody Against Putin’ Director’s Oscar Is Found After Airline Lost It
item.description: Pavel Talankin, a co-director and star of the documentary “Mr. Nobody Against Putin,” was told by T.S.A. agents that his Academy Award could be used as a weapon.
item.dc:creator: Gabe Castro-Root
item.media:description: Pavel Talankin won the Oscar for best documentary feature this year along with his co-director, David Borenstein.

item.title: How Good Is Google’s Gemini AI at Making Travel Plans?
item.description: Gemini is a digital Swiss Army knife for planning flights, activities and routes, but it isn’t perfect. Why did it forget to put underwear on the packing list?
item.dc:creator: Brian X. Chen

item.title: Uber Expands to Hotel Bookings With Expedia Partnership
item.description: In partnership with Expedia, the ride-booking, food-delivering, package-transporting service takes another step toward becoming a super app.
item.dc:creator: Tariro Mzezewa
item.media:credit: Ashley Gilbertson for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Park Lane Hotel in New York is one of the lodging options that can be booked through Uber.

item.title: What Travelers Need to Know About the EU’s New Biometric Border Checks
item.description: The rollout of new entry and exit checkpoints for travelers visiting 29 European countries has led to frustrating delays. Here’s what you need to know.
item.dc:creator: Christine Chung
item.media:description: Europe’s new Entry/Exit System was scheduled to be fully in place by April 10, but its debut has been inconsistent.

item.title: 5 Hotels for Your Next U.S. Road Trip
item.description: Add these new or newly revamped accommodations to your summer driving itinerary.
item.dc:creator: Stephanie Rosenbloom

item.title: Tractor-Trailer and Light Post Damaged as Plane Lands at Newark Airport
item.description: The tractor-trailer was on the New Jersey Turnpike on Sunday when it was damaged, the authorities said. Its driver had minor injuries and no one aboard the plane was hurt.
item.dc:creator: Alexandra E. Petri

item.title: What Is Hantavirus, Which Is Linked to the Deaths of 3 People Aboard a Cruise Ship?
item.description: Three people aboard a cruise ship sailing the Atlantic Ocean have died from what health officials suspect were hantavirus infections.
item.dc:creator: Livia Albeck-Ripka, Hank Sanders and Michael Levenson
item.media:description: Hantavirus most often spreads from dried rodent droppings.

item.title: 3 Dead in Hantavirus Outbreak Aboard Cruise Ship, W.H.O. Says
item.description: One case of hantavirus infection has been confirmed in a laboratory, the organization said on Sunday. There are five additional suspected cases.
item.dc:creator: Alexandra E. Petri and Livia Albeck-Ripka
item.media:description: The cruise ship MV Hondius off Cape Verde on Sunday. Officials identified the ship as the one with passengers infected by hantavirus.

item.title: Spirit Airlines’ Demise Could Help Other Airlines
item.description: Even in its reduced state, the company played an important role in forcing other airlines to keep fares low, some experts said.
item.dc:creator: Niraj Chokshi
item.media:credit: Tom Brenner for The New York Times
item.media:description: Spirit Airlines’ presence at an airport usually helped to push down the fares of all airlines operating there.

item.title: Driving Electric in Costa Rica Is Surprisingly Doable
item.description: The charging network is spotty, but it’s a small country.
item.dc:creator: Jack Ewing
item.media:description: Costa Rica requires utilities to install fast-charging stations every 50 miles on national highways. But many chargers don’t work.

item.title: Spirit Airlines Cancels All Flights, Stranding Passengers
item.description: The budget carrier abruptly canceled flights early on Saturday, leaving passengers to rush to make other plans. “Even if they go back into business, never again,” one traveler said.
item.dc:creator: Rylee Kirk
item.media:description: Some passengers were in the middle of their trips when they learned that their Spirit Airlines flights had been canceled.

item.title: Jordanians Struggle as Mideast Wars Scare Tourists Away
item.description: Though Jordan mostly sat out the conflicts, its tourism high season was nearly wiped out at popular sites. Visitors canceled flights, hotels and tours.
item.dc:creator: Raja Abdulrahim, Rana F. Sweis and Laura Boushnak
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: A few tourists in front of the 2,000-year-old Treasury building in the ancient city of Petra, Jordan, last month.

item.title: Spirit Airlines Shuts Down After Years of Struggle
item.description: Spirit once upended the industry by offering very low fares but was in its second bankruptcy in two years after years of struggle.
item.dc:creator: Niraj Chokshi
item.media:description: The Spirit Airlines ticket counter at Detroit Metropolitan Wayne County Airport in Romulus, Mich., was empty after the airline shut down overnight on Saturday.

item.title: Spirit Airlines Prepares to Shut Down After Trump Administration Bailout Falls Through
item.description: The low-fare airline, which has struggled for years, had been trying to secure a $500 million lifeline from the Trump administration.
item.dc:creator: Lauren Hirsch and Niraj Chokshi
item.media:description: Spirit Airlines will stop flying after failing to find a way to emerge from bankruptcy in a time of high fuel prices and stiff competition.

item.title: Iceland’s Pools and Hot Tubs Are Now UNESCO-Recognized. Some Locals Aren’t Thrilled.
item.description: Iceland’s swimming pools and hot tubs serve as hubs of social life, a cultural distinction recently honored by UNESCO. Some Icelanders aren’t so thrilled.
item.dc:creator: Amelia Nierenberg
item.category: United Nations Educational, Scientific and Cultural Organization
item.media:credit: Sergey Ponomarev for The New York Times

item.title: United Airlines CEO, Rebuffed by American, Says He’s Skeptical of Other Deals
item.description: The chief executive, Scott Kirby, said that buying a smaller airline might not be worth the effort.
item.dc:creator: Niraj Chokshi
item.media:credit: Patrick T. Fallon/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: “Doing a merger takes a lot of calories and a lot of energy,” Scott Kirby, chief executive of United Airlines, said in an interview.

item.title: The Murky Ethics of Swimming With Killer Whales
item.description: Only two places in the world allow tourists to enter the water with the ocean’s apex predator. But the safety of both species is a growing concern.
item.dc:creator: Alexa Robles-Gil and Meghan Dhaliwal
item.media:credit: Meghan Dhaliwal for The New York Times

item.title: The War in Iran Has Americans Rethinking Their Summer Travel Plans
item.description: Faced with geopolitical instability, economic uncertainty and rising airfares, many travelers are changing destinations or canceling their trips entirely.
item.dc:creator: Ceylan Yeğinsu
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Data from the aviation analytics firm Cirium shows a 10.5 percent decline in July bookings from the U.S. to Europe compared with last year.

item.title: Tight Curves and Wide Horizons: The Return of Highway 1
item.description: Once again, travelers can drive the full length of the scenic road in California after it was severed by a series of landslides starting in 2023.
item.dc:creator: Finn-Olaf Jones and Jason Henry
item.media:description: As of mid-February, drivers can once again travel the full length of Highway 1. Above, heading north on the highway, near San Simeon, Calif.

item.title: Budget Airlines Ask Trump Administration for Billions as Fuel Costs Rise
item.description: A trade group for the airlines is seeking $2.5 billion to help offset the big jump in jet fuel costs since the start of U.S.-Israeli attacks against Iran.
item.dc:creator: Karoun Demirjian and Lauren Hirsch
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: A trade group that represents low-cost carriers including Frontier Airlines is seeking $2.5 billion from the Trump administration to offset some fuel costs.

item.title: United Airlines Says It Pitched American on Merger but Was Rebuffed
item.description: United’s chief acknowledged for the first time that he had broached the idea of a merger with American, which was unwilling to consider it.
item.dc:creator: Niraj Chokshi
item.media:credit: Patrick T. Fallon/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Scott Kirby, chief of United Airlines, said a merger with American Airlines would have generated significant economic benefits for the United States.

item.title: Paddling the Threatened Boundary Waters
item.description: A proposed copper mine in northern Minnesota has become a battleground for politicians and environmentalists — and a pressing reason to explore the waterways.
item.dc:creator: Stephanie Pearson
item.media:credit: Aaron Lavinsky/Star Tribune, via Getty Images
